story:
Lily was annoyed. Her mom had flipped her virtual reality helmet into school mode, so while Lily’s friends lounged on some Australian or Brazilian beach, she was stuck in her room until homework was done. She was thirteen and didn’t care about a history class about what happened thirty years ago. In those ancient times, people actually had to travel to see a place.

Her teacher had said that people then even touched one another just to say hello, and some were careless enough to share a bed. Lily brushed them off as savages. No wonder they were defenseless when the virus came.

For homework, Lily had to research her family’s story from those days. It was optional, but her mom insisted. For reasons Lily didn’t understand, her mom seemed permanently attached to that time. Sighing, Lily tapped open her iPad 29, synced with the helmet, hunted down an ancient app called Instagram, and opened her mom’s account. Since 2030, every moment of life had been recorded, so anyone could put on a helmet and replay their life in 3D with friends and family. But in the 20s people still posted pictures of “important moments.”

Lily scrolled to 2020. There was her mom, very young, in a black square hat, sitting by an old-school computer. Several squares were tiled across the screen, each showing a young, smiling guy in the same hat. Many had beards—a barbaric, unhygienic male trend of those days. The caption said: “My graduation party during the lockdown. Looking forward to the day when I can hug everyone.” Unbelievable. Back then, keeping your physical body safe was considered limiting. And “hug”? Lily knew the word but not the point. Why touch all those people? Was it some risky way to check for fever?

She watched a few explanatory videos. Apparently people hugged to express emotion. As if emojis weren’t enough. True, emojis are much more advanced now—holograms in VR and in real life—but even then people used them in chats.

With another sigh, Lily wrote her homework: When the virus came, my mom was a young, reckless rebel. She urged people to meet in person and promoted physical contact. Even on her graduation day, instead of celebrating responsibly, she wanted to see her friends. My mom is very smart, but even she didn’t grasp the danger of being social. Her behavior shows how careless and naive people were before the World Restriction Law passed in 2023. I’m glad my mom changed; she would never touch me or anyone else because she knows how unsafe it is. Dangerous, unhealthy times are gone; we live in a brave new world.

“Mom, my homework is done, can I please go?” Lily said to the empty room, knowing “mom” would ping her. Her mom connected, nodded virtually, and switched Lily’s helmet to leisure mode. Instantly Lily joined her friends, who were hanging out on a volcano rim in Hawaii.

Her mom stayed behind. Like all parents, she could access her child’s notes. She opened Lily’s assignment, saw the old Instagram shot, and wondered if Lily realized one of the tiny squares in that Zoom grid was her father. Probably not. Lily was born after beards were banned; she couldn’t imagine her dad with one. Then the mom read the essay. If anyone else had been in her room, they would have seen a woman sitting very still in a helmet. Even through the visor, they would have noticed a strange expression. Someone from her generation would have called it crying. But how could that be? People of the bright new age don’t express emotions. They use emojis instead.
